title: The Lord's Weeping over Jerusalem
subtitle: Christ draws near the city, and through his tears foresees its ruin

stillness: he taught us to weep rather than to laugh.
and let the noise of your gladness fall silent.
gospel: As he drew near and saw the city,
the things that are for your peace —
but now they are hidden from your eyes.
For the days shall come upon you
when your enemies will cast a rampart about you,
and surround you, and hem you in on every side,
and they shall dash you to the ground,
and they shall not leave in you one stone upon another,
because you did not know the time of your visitation.
behold: Behold attentively, as if you were present:
the Lord draws near to Jerusalem —
not only with the step of his body,
but with the feeling of his compassion.
See how, while the crowd pours out its song,
he himself takes up a lament over the ruin of the city.
Look not only with the eye of the body
upon the city and its outward beauty,
but with the eye of his divinity
he beholds the impious city conspiring for his death,
foreseeing its fall and its overthrow.
He was the fountain of mercy,
and therefore he could not hold back his tears.
He did not weep for the walls of the buildings,
but for the overthrow of souls.
consider: He wept not over his own death, which they were plotting,
but over their ruin and their perdition.
He wept for the evils of guilt, multiplied within that place,
and for the evils of punishment soon to fall upon city and people.
Human gladness is turned swiftly into weeping;
all the joys and honors of the world
are mingled with tears and with sorrows.
In heaven all things are pure;
in the world all things are mixed;
in hell all things are mournful.
Worldly gladness is like pure wine,
which quickly makes drunk, unless it be tempered —
tempered by the remembrance of death.
Now you rejoice, because you do not see what is coming.
The present prosperity keeps the coming adversity unconsidered,
for the joy of the hypocrite is like a single point.
Understand this in the moral sense:
this city is the soul of the sinner,
over whom Christ weeps with the weeping of compassion.
If the soul would only consider the state of its guilt,
and what state of punishment it awaits in time to come,
it would bewail itself together with Christ.
But the delight of sin takes away
the thought of sudden death,
the fear of the strict judgment,
and the dread of the pains of hell.
What the Lord once did according to history,
this he does daily through his elect in the Church:
he weeps over the reprobate, who know not why they are wept for,
but exult and rejoice in their evils.
prayer: who by word and by example
taught us to weep rather than to laugh,
and by all your acts of mercy,
teach me always to see and to know
my sins and the dangers that press upon me;
lest my enemies — the suggestions of demons,
the beauties of temporal things, and the pleasures of the flesh —
surround me, hem me in, and dash me to the ground;
lest they destroy my children — that is, my senses,
my thoughts and my acts, and their right order;
that thereby I may praise you,
and confess your name, O Most High. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Fons enim pietatis erat, et ideo lacrymas continere non poterat, nec ædificia planxit parietum, sed subversionem animarum.
latinJewel.english: For he was the fountain of mercy, and therefore he could not hold back his tears; nor did he weep for the walls of the buildings, but for the overthrow of souls.
